title: Experimentally probing the Quantum Physics in the Inverted Harmonic Oscillator
authors: Si-Cong Ji, Philipp Schüttelkopf, Nataliia Bazhan, Federica Cataldini, Mohammadamin Tajik, Frederik S. Møller, Igor Mazets, Sebastian Erne, Jörg Schmiedmayer
abstract: When a quantum system passes through an unstable fixed point the local dynamics reduces to the inverted harmonic oscillator (IHO). It exponentially amplifies along one quadrature while squeezing the other, producing macroscopically extended quantum states from microscopic zero-point fluctuations. We realize this dynamics with a Bose-Einstein condensate on an AtomChip. Radio-frequency dressing flips the transverse harmonic confinement into an IHO. Through phase-space tomography we follow the full Wigner function of the evolving quantum state, observe sub-vacuum squeezing of 10.6(1.3) dB, and test coherent reversibility by time-reversing the IHO evolution. Matter-wave interference between the two daughter clouds confirms quantum coherence over timescales far beyond the initial expansion. Our experiment establishes ultra-cold atoms as a clean, controlled, many-body platform for unstable quantum dynamics opening a route to force sensing with time-reversal-based coherence certification and to analog studies of the amplification of quantum fluctuations in inflationary field dynamics.
summary: The researchers used a Bose-Einstein condensate on an AtomChip to simulate the dynamics of an Inverted Harmonic Oscillator (IHO), which models unstable quantum evolution. They measured sub-vacuum squeezing and confirmed that the quantum state retains coherence even after undergoing rapid, amplifying expansion. This work establishes ultra-cold atoms as a powerful tool for studying fundamental quantum phenomena relevant to cosmology and quantum sensing.
main_problem: The paper experimentally investigates the quantum dynamics governed by the Inverted Harmonic Oscillator (IHO), a model for unstable quantum evolution, using ultra-cold atoms.
main_result: They successfully demonstrated sub-vacuum squeezing and confirmed the coherent reversibility of the IHO dynamics using a Bose-Einstein condensate, establishing a clean platform for studying quantum fluctuations.
method: The dynamics are probed using phase-space tomography on the Wigner function, and coherence is verified via matter-wave interference and time-reversal protocols.
model_or_system: The system is a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) trapped on an AtomChip, where the transverse confinement is dynamically quenched via RF dressing to approximate an IHO potential. The dynamics are analyzed using the Gross-Pitaevskii equation (GPE) in the many-body limit.
key_observables: Sub-vacuum squeezing (up to 10.6 dB), Wigner function evolution, matter-wave interference contrast, and the decay/restoration of quantum purity.
important_parameters: Effective IHO frequency ($\omega_I = 2\pi 	imes 0.9$ kHz), initial transverse confinement ($\omega_\perp = 2\pi 	imes 2.1$ kHz), and time-of-flight measurement times ($t_E$).
main_assumptions: The single-mode IHO description is assumed accurate for short times, though the experiment probes regimes where interaction-induced mode coupling becomes significant.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup (RF quench), the time evolution sequence including time-reversal (loop protocol), and the resulting matter-wave interference patterns and momentum distributions used for tomography.
paper_structure: The paper follows a structure of realizing the IHO potential via RF dressing, characterizing the resulting quantum state using Wigner tomography, demonstrating the exponential amplification and squeezing, and finally testing the coherence and reversibility via a time-reversal loop protocol.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly controlled, many-body quantum simulator for fundamental processes like cosmological inflation, offering quantitative measurements of quantum noise amplification and coherence preservation in an ultracold atomic setting.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Preparation of the IHO initial condition. The RF quench transforms the transverse harmonic confinement into a double-well potential whose central barrier is locally ap- proximated by an inverted harmonic oscillator. The atoms are initially localized at the former trap minimum, which co- incides with the unstable fixed point of the IHO.

FIG. 3. Coherent expansion probed by matter-wave in- terference. (a) The BEC expands in the inverted harmonic potential from the top of the barrier into the double well. The split cloud is released close to the far turning point. The wave functions expand and overlap in time-of-flight (ToF). (b) Interference pattern of the two daughter clouds split by the barrier after a 5.5 ms hold, integrated along the longitu- dinal (z) direction. (c) Distribution of the phase φ extracted from the fringes. The fringe contrast C = 0.24(0.04), together with the pronounced peak in φ rather than a uniform distri- bution, demonstrate that phase coherence between the two copies survives the full inflation.

FIG. 2. (a) Time sequence of the coherent expansion. (b) Time sequence for restoring the initial coherent state. For both figures, the upper panel shows the potential at differ- ent stages of the experiment, while the lower panel shows the corresponding phase-space distributions. The dark (light) red ellipses represent the Wigner distribution at the end (begin- ning) of each stage.

FIG. 4. (a) Momentum distributions at different tomogra- phy times for the expanded state with tE = 0.1 ms. Lower- left panel: after ToF the atomic density is integrated along y, and the momentum distribution along the expanded direc- tion is fitted (red line) to extract its width and variance. (b) Evolution of the momentum variance, fitted with a damped- oscillation model (blue line); the red line is the corresponding undamped oscillation. The numbers mark the time points in panel (a). (c) Wigner distribution reconstructed from (b) by inverse-Radon transformation, for tE = 0.1 ms.

FIG. 5. (a) Wigner distributions of the expanded states reconstructed by inverse-Radon transformation for varying expansion times. (b) Momentum (blue) and position (red) variances of the expanded states versus expansion time. (c) Squeeze factor versus expansion time. (d) Orientation angle φ of the major axis of the Wigner ellipse versus expansion time tE. The asymptotic angle determined by ωI/ω⊥is shown by the dashed line. (e) Purity PG of the reconstructed Gaussian state versus expansion time. The reduced PG(0) ∼0.75 for the BEC results from interactions in the ground state; the ap- parent loss of purity with time, present even for a single par- ticle, is the result of the anharmonicity of...
